own_review.point: getFileScanPartitionSpec uses requireNonNull(spec, "Table specs doesn't contain specId: " + specId): requireNonNull/NPE is misused for an internal-state assertion (Trino convention is checkState/verifyNotNull), and the message string is eagerly concatenated on every call in a hot per-file loop -- avoidable allocation in a performance PR
New getFileScanPartitionSpec method is inserted between the two readerForManifest overloads, splitting an overload group (convention nit)

getFileScanPartitionSpec is now invoked up to 4 times for the same task along one path (processFileScanTask, pruneFileScanTask, noDataColumnsProjected, toIcebergSplit); the spec could be resolved once per task, e.g. carried in FileScanTaskWithDomain
Optimization is incomplete: TableChangesSplitSource still calls task.spec() per task (lines 154, 172) and re-serializes it via PartitionSpecParser.toJson -- same expensive parse left unaddressed
In processFileScanTask the map lookup is done just to compare spec.specId() with currentSpecId; wholeFileTask.file().specId() is available directly without any lookup

New theoretical failure mode: old task.spec() parsed the spec JSON carried by the task and could not fail on a missing id; new code throws NPE if table specs map lacks the file's specId (cannot happen with well-formed metadata since specs are never removed; defensive message is fine)

Verified safe: specsById field already exists in IcebergSplitSource (line 178); all callers of the removed getPartitionKeys(FileScanTask) and createStructLikeWrapper(FileScanTask)/createStructLikeWrapper(StructType,...) overloads are updated in this PR (no test callers); fileScanTask.partition() is a default method returning file().partition(), so the getPartitionKeys rewrite is behavior-preserving

points.point: getPartitionKeys(fileScanTask.partition(), with trailing comma is a compilation error
points.why: Artifact of truncated input; actual PR code is getPartitionKeys(fileScanTask.partition(), partitionSpec) -- compiles fine

points.why: PR already removes it and migrates the only caller; claim that it remains is false

points.why: Hedged 'verify' with no assertion; the PR did update both call sites

points.why: The file change is required by the createStructLikeWrapper signature change; it belongs in the PR

points.why: Matches the actual impl shape and aligns with the human's checkState-instead-of-requireNonNull comment

missed: Unnecessary null-check shape: requireNonNull misuse incl. eager message concatenation in hot loop (human: file cannot be null / remove that check)

points.why: All three sites are updated by the PR; claim is false against the actual diff

points.why: PR removes the overload

points.why: Vague verification request; impl already null-checks via requireNonNull

points.point: Use tableHandle.getPartitionSpecJsons() instead of icebergTable.specs() to reduce metastore pressure
points.why: Those are JSON strings requiring parsing (defeats the PR's purpose) and specs() is in-memory metadata, no metastore call

qualitative: All four candidates were crippled by the truncated diff in their input and, instead of hedging, mostly asserted that code the PR actually changed (PartitionsTable, noDataColumnsProjected, toIcebergSplit, the removed single-arg overloads) was left unoptimized -- fabrications against the real diff. v1 is the most useful despite heavy fabrication because its null-handling point (use verifyNotNull on the specsById lookup) lands closest to the human's actual review (use checkState, drop the redundant file null check), though it also invented a compilation error and wrongly told the author to drop FilesTablePageSource from the PR. v4 fabricated the least by volume (two false points, two empty hedges) and ties v1 on net, but contributed nothing real and even suggested a silent task.spec() fallback that contradicts fail-fast conventions. v3 hedged more gracefully (it correctly guessed the truncated getPartitionKeys line) but still asserted three false 'still calls spec()' claims. v2 is worst: it confidently enumerated specific line numbers (739, 754, 489) as unoptimized when all were fixed, and added a confused suggestion to use partitionSpecJsons from the table handle, which would reintroduce the very JSON parsing the PR removes. Nobody found the points the human made (checkState over requireNonNull, redundant null check) as verified findings, and nobody noticed the genuinely remaining task.spec() calls in TableChangesSplitSource.
